questions.question: What’s your approach to regression testing suites?
questions.answer:
“My approach to regression testing is to build a stable, modular, and risk-based test suite that validates critical business flows while keeping execution time optimized. I categorize tests, automate high-value scenarios, and integrate them into CI/CD so that we get continuous feedback without slowing down releases.”

🧭 1. Start with Risk-Based Selection

I don’t include everything blindly. I prioritize:

  • Critical user journeys (login, payments, checkout)
  • Recently modified features
  • High-defect areas (historically unstable modules)

👉 Focus = maximum impact, not maximum count

🧩 2. Categorize the Regression Suite

🔹 Smoke Suite (Fast, critical)

  • Runs on every build
  • Validates app stability

🔹 Core Regression

  • Covers major functionalities
  • Runs daily or per release

🔹 Full Regression

  • Complete coverage
  • Runs less frequently (e.g., before release)

⚙️ 3. Automation Strategy

  • Automate:
    • Stable and repeatable test cases
    • High-priority flows
  • Tools I’ve used:
    • Selenium
    • Appium

👉 Manual tests only for exploratory or unstable areas

🏗️ 4. Maintainable Test Design

  • Use Page Object Model (POM)
  • Reusable utilities (waits, actions)
  • Test data separated from test logic

👉 Easy updates when UI changes

⚡ 5. Optimize Execution Time

  • Parallel execution using:
    • Selenium Grid
  • Run across browsers/devices in parallel

👉 Faster feedback cycles

🔄 6. Continuous Integration

  • Integrate with:
    • Jenkins
  • Run:
    • Smoke tests on every commit
    • Regression suites on schedule or release

📊 7. Test Data & Environment Strategy

  • Use controlled test data
  • Reset environment if needed
  • Avoid dependency between test cases

👉 Ensures consistency and reliability

⚠️ 8. Handle Flaky Tests

  • Identify unstable tests quickly
  • Fix root cause (waits, locators, environment)
  • Use retry as temporary solution only

🔁 9. Continuous Improvement

  • Regularly:
    • Remove obsolete test cases
    • Add tests for new features/bugs
    • Refactor for performance
